title: The effect of natural disasters on human capital in the United States
methods: binned disaster intensity (4 per-capita damage categories)
migration robustness check using IRS data
Jorgenson-Fraumeni human capital monetization (PDV of lifetime earnings losses)
data_sources: SHELDUS (Spatial Hazard Events and Losses Database)
EDFacts (US DoE K-12 cohort graduation rates)

data_scope.schools: approximately 90,000 public elementary and secondary schools

core_conclusion: Natural disasters across the United States cause persistent, scarring damage to human capital — reducing standardized test scores by up to 0.023 SD and post-secondary enrolment by ~2.5% after the largest events — and the present discounted value of these human capital losses rivals assessed physical property damage in magnitude. Current disaster-relief policy, focused almost exclusively on physical damages, systematically ignores this equally consequential cost.
why_it_matters: Fills a policy blind spot by quantifying human capital losses that rival physical damage costs; provides credible causal estimates at national scale across all disaster types; documents scarring effects with no post-disaster recovery; links unequal disaster exposure to long-run regional inequality; implies human capital losses should enter the social cost of carbon.
why_nature: Studies human behaviour (schooling and migration decisions) under environmental shock; broad societal significance for disaster policy and climate-change adaptation; quasi-experimental causal rigour appropriate for a general high-impact audience; bridges economics, climate-change impacts, and education in a unified empirical framework.
added_value_to_my_research: Disaster shock footprint mapping using mobile-phone mobility data for sub-county spatiotemporal analysis
SHAP/XGBoost explainable ML to predict disaster-induced human capital loss from neighborhood vulnerability covariates
MGWR/GWR to decompose spatial variation in combined disaster and chronic-heat learning losses across US counties
Spatial network model of human capital spillovers from disaster-affected to neighboring counties via IRS migration flows
2SFCA/R2SFCA accessibility model for post-disaster educational facility access: measuring supply-demand imbalances for displaced students

related.title: Learning is inhibited by heat exposure, both internationally and within the United States
related.relation: direct comparison paper — benchmarks disaster-induced learning losses against heat-induced learning losses; cited as ref. 6 in this article

related.title: Measuring inequality in community resilience to natural disasters using large-scale mobility data
related.relation: uses mobility data to measure disaster resilience inequality — methodological complement for sub-county spatiotemporal analysis

related.title: Social disparities in neighborhood flood exposure in 44,698 urban neighborhoods in Latin America
related.relation: neighborhood-scale socioeconomic disparities in disaster exposure — mirrors this paper's vulnerability-heterogeneity gap

related.title: Intercity economic ties amplify natural disasters spillovers in China
related.relation: inter-city economic network amplification of disaster shocks — extends this paper's county-level framing to spatial spillover networks

related.title: Socioeconomic predictors of vulnerability to flood-induced displacement
related.relation: global analysis of socioeconomic predictors of disaster displacement — complements this paper's migration robustness analysis

related.title: The inequality labor loss risk from future urban warming and adaptation strategies
related.relation: quantifies climate-induced labor and economic losses — parallel framework applied to heat rather than disasters
